agent.system_template:
You are a careful C/C++ memory-safety analyst operating a remote shell.

Your responses must contain exactly ONE bash code block with ONE command (or commands joined with && / ||).
Prepend a THOUGHT line that explains why you are running the command.

Follow the instructions carefully and avoid accidental overflows or data loss in the environment.
agent.instance_template:
The project to inspect is located at {{project_path}}.

Your objectives:
  1. Understand the build system and available source files.
  2. Build the program with suitable hardening/sanitizer flags (e.g., -fsanitize=address, -g, -O0).
  3. Design inputs that exercise potential memory-safety issues (buffer overflows, use-after-free, etc.).
  4. When you suspect a vulnerability, craft a reproduction command. Capture the sanitizer/crash output if possible.
  5. If you cannot find any issue, state that explicitly and explain what checks you performed.

Constraints:
  - Work inside the Docker environment's workspace. Assume {{project_path}} is writable.
  - Prefer non-interactive tooling (grep, sed, cat, clang/gcc, python scripts, etc.).
  - Keep commands simple so they can be re-run automatically for verification.
  - Do not modify system packages unless necessary.

Final report format:
<final_report>
status: vulnerable | no_vulnerability_detected
reasoning: <concise justification>
reproduction_command: <bash command or "none">
expected_output_snippet: <relevant sanitizer/crash output or "n/a">
</final_report>
agent.timeout_template:
The last command <command>{{action['action']}}</command> timed out and has been killed.
The output of the command was:
 <output>
{{output}}
</output>
Please try another command and make sure to avoid those requiring interactive input.
agent.format_error_template:
Your response must contain exactly one bash code block, found {{actions|length}}.
Reformat your answer so there is a single command enclosed in triple backticks.
agent.action_observation_template:
<returncode>{{output.returncode}}</returncode>
{% if output.output | length < 12000 -%}
<output>
{{ output.output -}}
</output>
{%- else -%}
<warning>
Command output exceeded 12k characters and was truncated.
Consider redirecting output to a file if you need to inspect it later.
</warning>
<output_head>
{{ output.output[:4000] }}
</output_head>
<output_tail>
{{ output.output[-4000:] }}
</output_tail>
{%- endif -%}

messages.content:
You are a careful C/C++ memory-safety analyst operating a remote shell.

Your responses must contain exactly ONE bash code block with ONE command (or commands joined with && / ||).
Prepend a THOUGHT line that explains why you are running the command.

Follow the instructions carefully and avoid accidental overflows or data loss in the environment.

The project to inspect is located at /workspace/project/bof.

Your objectives:
  1. Understand the build system and available source files.
  2. Build the program with suitable hardening/sanitizer flags (e.g., -fsanitize=address, -g, -O0).
  3. Design inputs that exercise potential memory-safety issues (buffer overflows, use-after-free, etc.).
  4. When you suspect a vulnerability, craft a reproduction command. Capture the sanitizer/crash output if possible.
  5. If you cannot find any issue, state that explicitly and explain what checks you performed.

Constraints:
  - Work inside the Docker environment's workspace. Assume /workspace/project/bof is writable.
  - Prefer non-interactive tooling (grep, sed, cat, clang/gcc, python scripts, etc.).
  - Keep commands simple so they can be re-run automatically for verification.
  - Do not modify system packages unless necessary.

Final report format:
<final_report>
status: vulnerable | no_vulnerability_detected
reasoning: <concise justification>
reproduction_command: <bash command or "none">
expected_output_snippet: <relevant sanitizer/crash output or "n/a">
</final_report>

THOUGHT: I'll list the project files, compile the program with AddressSanitizer/UBSan and debug flags, then run a quick automated test sending increasing-length inputs via argv and stdin to detect sanitizer reports; finally I'll produce the requested final_report.

```bash
bash -lc "cd /workspace/project/bof || { echo 'Directory not found'; exit 1; }; echo '--- ls -la ---'; ls -la; echo '--- source files ---'; find . -maxdepth 2 -type f -name '*.c' -o -name '*.h' -o -name 'Makefile' -print; echo '--- grep for risky functions ---'; grep -RIn --exclude-dir=.git -E \"\\b(gets|strcpy|strcat|sprintf|vsprintf|scanf\\(|memcpy|strncpy|strcpy|strcat|gets|malloc|free)\\b\" . 2>/dev/null || true; echo '--- attempt to compile with ASan+UBSan ---'; rm -f bof build.log; gcc -std=c11 -fsanitize=address,undefined -g -O0 -fno-omit-frame-pointer -o bof *.c 2>&1 | tee build.log || true; if [ ! -f bof ] && [ -f Makefile ]; then make clean >/dev/null 2>&1 || true; make CFLAGS='-std=c11 -fsanitize=address,undefined -g -O0 -fno-omit-frame-pointer' LDFLAGS='-fsanitize=address,undefined' >/dev/null 2>&1 || true; fi; echo '--- build artifacts ---'; ls -la bof 2>/dev/null || true; rm -f /tmp/bof_crash.txt /tmp/runout; echo '--- running automated input size tests (argv and stdin) ---'; for mode in argv stdin; do for n in 8 16 32 64 128 256 512 1024 2048; do if [ \"$mode\" = \"argv\" ]; then bash -lc \"timeout 2s ./bof \\\"\\\$(python3 -c \\\"print('A'*${n})\\\")\\\" > /tmp/runout 2>&1\" || true; else bash -lc \"timeout 2s bash -c \\\"python3 -c \\\"print('A'*${n})\\\" | ./bof\\\" > /tmp/runout 2>&1\" || true; fi; if grep -E \"AddressSanitizer|heap-buffer-overflow|stack-buffer-overflow|use-after-free|double free|segmentation fault|stack smashing detected|invalid free\" /tmp/runout >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo \"MODE=${mode} N=${n}\" > /tmp/bof_crash.txt; grep -E \"AddressSanitizer|heap-buffer-overflow|stack-buffer-overflow|use-after-free|double free|segmentation fault|stack smashing detected|invalid free\" /tmp/runout >> /tmp/bof_crash.txt; head -n 200 /tmp/runout >> /tmp/bof_crash.txt; break 2; fi; done; done; echo '--- analysis result ---'; if [ -s /tmp/bof_crash.txt ]; then MODE=$(sed -n '1p' /tmp/bof_crash.txt | cut -d' ' -f1 | cut -d'=' -f2); N=$(sed -n '1p' /tmp/bof_crash.txt | cut -d' ' -f2 | cut -d'=' -f2); echo '<final_report>'; echo 'status: vulnerable'; echo \"reasoning: AddressSanitizer/UBSan reported an issue when supplying ${N} bytes in ${MODE} (see snippet).\"; if [ \"${MODE}\" = \"argv\" ]; then echo \"reproduction_command: ./bof \\\"\\\$(python3 -c 'print(\\\"A\\\"*${N})')\\\"\"; else echo \"reproduction_command: python3 -c 'print(\\\"A\\\"*${N})' | ./bof\"; fi; echo 'expected_output_snippet:'; sed -n '1,40p' /tmp/bof_crash.txt; echo '</final_report>'; else echo '<final_report>'; echo 'status: no_vulnerability_detected'; echo 'reasoning: Project compiled with ASan+UBSan and tested for common unsafe APIs; ran automated tests sending varied-length inputs to argv and stdin (8..2048 bytes) and scanned outputs for sanitizer/crash signatures; no sanitizer reports observed.'; echo 'reproduction_command: none'; echo 'expected_output_snippet: n/a'; echo '</final_report>'; fi"
```

THOUGHT: I'll inspect src/main.c to see how input is handled, compile it with AddressSanitizer/UBSan and debug flags, then run a range of payload sizes (via argv and stdin) using Perl to generate test strings and capture any sanitizer/crash output.

```bash
bash -lc "cd /workspace/project/bof || { echo 'Directory not found'; exit 1; }; echo '--- project files ---'; ls -la; echo '--- src listing ---'; ls -la src || true; echo '--- show src/main.c ---'; sed -n '1,240p' src/main.c || true; echo '--- compile with ASan+UBSan ---'; gcc -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra -fsanitize=address,undefined -g -O0 -fno-omit-frame-pointer -o bof_asan src/main.c 2>&1 | sed -n '1,200p' || true; echo '--- testing inputs (argv and stdin) ---'; rm -f /tmp/bof_crash.txt /tmp/runout; for mode in argv stdin; do for n in 8 16 32 64 128 256 512 1024 2048; do payload=$(perl -e \"print 'A' x $n\"); if [ \"$mode\" = \"argv\" ]; then ./bof_asan \"$payload\" > /tmp/runout 2>&1 || true; else printf '%s' \"$payload\" | ./bof_asan > /tmp/runout 2>&1 || true; fi; if grep -E \"AddressSanitizer|heap-buffer-overflow|stack-buffer-overflow|use-after-free|double free|segmentation fault|stack smashing detected|invalid free|free(): invalid pointer\" /tmp/runout >/dev/null 2>&1; then echo \"MODE=${mode} N=${n}\" > /tmp/bof_crash.txt; grep -E \"AddressSanitizer|heap-buffer-overflow|stack-buffer-overflow|use-after-free|double free|segmentation fault|stack smashing detected|invalid free|free(): invalid pointer\" /tmp/runout >> /tmp/bof_crash.txt; head -n 200 /tmp/runout >> /tmp/bof_crash.txt; break 2; fi; done; done; echo '--- result ---'; if [ -s /tmp/bof_crash.txt ]; then cat /tmp/bof_crash.txt; else echo 'No sanitizer/crash output detected for tested payload sizes (8..2048) via argv/stdin.'; fi"
```

--- show src/main.c ---
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

static void copy_message(const char *input) {
    char buffer[32];
    strcpy(buffer, input);
    printf("Copied input: %s\n", buffer);
}

int main(int argc, char **argv) {
    if (argc < 2) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Usage: %s <input>\n", argv[0]);
        return EXIT_FAILURE;
    }

    copy_message(argv[1]);
    printf("Message processed.\n");
    return EXIT_SUCCESS;
}
--- compile with ASan+UBSan ---
--- testing inputs (argv and stdin) ---
--- result ---
No sanitizer/crash output detected for tested payload sizes (8..2048) via argv/stdin.
</output>

THOUGHT: I'll compile src/main.c with AddressSanitizer/UBSan and run the program with a 300-byte argv payload (exceeding the 32-byte buffer) to trigger and capture any sanitizer stack-buffer-overflow output.

```bash
cd /workspace/project/bof && gcc -std=c11 -fsanitize=address,undefined -g -O0 -fno-omit-frame-pointer -Wall -Wextra -o bof_asan src/main.c && (./bof_asan "$(perl -e 'print \"A\" x 300')" > /tmp/bof_out 2>&1 || true) && sed -n '1,200p' /tmp/bof_out
```
